meta.title: E46/5 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 3 Series Compact E46/5 engine — N42, N46, N40, N45, M54 and M47. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the four‑cylinder vs six‑cylinder choice explained. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

@graph.name: BMW 3 Series Compact E46/5 Engine Codes
BMW 3 Series Compact E46/5 Core Variants

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 3 Series Compact E46/5 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E46/5 engine typically costs £1,400–£4,500 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on engine — the 1.6‑litre four‑cylinder petrol sits at £1,400–£2,600, while the M54B25 straight‑six can reach £2,500–£4,500. Fitting adds £520–£910. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which E46/5 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M54B25 2.5‑litre straight‑six (325ti) — a naturally aspirated, proven engine from the E46‑era BMWs — is widely regarded as the most reliable E46/5 engine. It delivers 192 hp, superb refinement, and has a proven 150,000‑mile reputation when properly maintained. The M47D20TÜ 2.0‑litre diesel (320td) is also a solid, torque‑rich choice with 150 hp and excellent fuel economy.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 3 Series Compact E46/5?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain and VANOS wear on the N42/N46 four‑cylinder petrols (rattle on start‑up is the warning), cooling system failures on the M54 straight‑six (overheating risk), injector and turbo wear on the M47 diesel, and — unrelated to the engine — rear subframe mount corrosion and tailgate wiring loom fractures on all E46/5s.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 3 Series Compact E46/5 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on the engine and maintenance history. The M54 straight‑six is the most reliable — it has proven durability and is well‑supported by the BMW specialist community. The M47 diesel is also solid. The N42/N46 four‑cylinder petrols introduced Valvetronic technology and won International Engine of the Year in 2001, but the timing chain and VANOS can be issues — a documented service history is essential. A well‑maintained E46/5 with the right engine can be a reliable compact hatchback.

mainEntity.name: Which E46/5 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the N42/N46 four‑cylinder petrols without documented timing chain or VANOS history require careful inspection. The M54 straight‑six is the safest choice for both daily driving and long‑term ownership. The 1.6‑litre N40/N45 is underpowered for the Compact's weight but simple and cheap to run.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E46/5 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs: rattle on cold start (N42/N46 timing chain), overheating or coolant loss (M54 cooling system), rough idle or black smoke (M47 injector/turbo issues), or loss of power and poor starting (any engine). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an E46/5 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £7,000 E46/5, a £2,500–£4,500 M54 recon is 35–65% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £3,500 example, the same recon is 70–130% — walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: if the lowest viable replacement cost exceeds 70% of the vehicle's current market value, evaluate scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How long do E46/5 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M54 straight‑six routinely exceeds 150,000–200,000 miles with proper servicing (regular oil changes, cooling system maintenance). The M47 diesel is similarly durable. The N42/N46 four‑cylinder petrols can also reach high mileages but the timing chain is the limiting factor — addressed proactively, they can comfortably reach 150,000+ miles.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E46/5 and the E36 Compact?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E36 Compact (1994–2000) was the first‑generation 3 Series Compact — based on the E36 platform with a unique front end. The E46/5 (2001–2004, this page) is the second‑generation Compact — based on the E46 platform, 21 cm shorter than the E46 saloon, with the E30‑derived trailing‑arm rear axle. The E46/5 was replaced by the BMW 1 Series (E87) in 2004. Costs and reliability profiles differ — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW E46/5 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine number is stamped on the block — on four‑cylinder engines (N42, N46, N40, N45, M47) it's on the left‑hand side; on the M54 straight‑six it's on the front of the block near the timing cover. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW 3 Series Compact E46/5 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E46‑based 3 Series Compact — from the Valvetronic N42 four‑cylinder petrols to the six‑cylinder M54 in the 325ti and the M47 diesel. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for BMW's compact rear‑wheel‑drive hatchback. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 3 Series Compact E46/5 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E46‑based Compact (E46/5) specifically — from the April 2000 launch to the December 2004 end of production.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any E46/5 engine family. Ratings are derived from triangulated specialist sources (BMW forums, owner clubs, AustralianCar.Reviews). The M54B25 straight‑six is widely regarded as the most reliable of the E46/5 line‑up — a proven, naturally aspirated engine with a strong reputation. The N42/N46 four‑cylinders introduced Valvetronic technology and won International Engine of the Year in 2001, but can suffer from timing chain and VANOS issues. The M47 diesel is robust and proven, with the 150 hp 320td being the pick of the diesel range.

overview.intro: The E46/5 is the E46‑based 3 Series Compact — a three‑door hatchback launched in June 2001 as BMW's entry‑level model. Based on the E46 platform but 21 cm shorter than the saloon, it featured a unique front fascia with distinctive headlights and an E30‑derived trailing‑arm rear axle. Under the bonnet, the Compact offered a choice of four‑cylinder petrol engines with BMW's new Valvetronic technology — the 1.8‑litre 316ti and 2.0‑litre 318ti — plus the six‑cylinder 325ti and M47 diesel options. Production ran until December 2004, when the Compact was replaced by the BMW 1 Series (E87).
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW E46 (rear‑wheel drive) • Predecessor: BMW 3 Series Compact (E36/5) • Successor: BMW 1 Series (E87) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (E46/5 is 3‑door hatchback only; E46 saloon, coupé, touring, convertible are separate models).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The E46/5 generates consistent UK enquiries [PATTERN DATA], concentrated on 316ti and 318ti petrol variants as these entry‑level BMWs enter affordable‑maintenance territory.

items.quote: Most dependable: The M54B25 2.5‑litre straight‑six (325ti) — a naturally aspirated, proven engine from the E46‑era BMWs — delivers 192 hp, superb refinement, and a genuine 150,000‑mile reputation when properly maintained. The M47D20TÜ 2.0‑litre diesel (320td) is also a solid, torque‑rich choice with 150 hp and excellent fuel economy. The N42/N46 four‑cylinder petrols are efficient and feature Valvetronic technology but can suffer from timing chain and VANOS issues; the 316ti's 1.8‑litre engine won International Engine of the Year in 2001. Buyers should prioritise a well‑maintained 325ti or 320td with documented service history.

ownershipEconomics.h2: BMW 3 Series Compact E46/5 — Ownership Economics

rows.ownershipVerdict: Solid Four — more power, same risks
Best Petrol — the Compact's most complete engine
Best Diesel — 150 hp, VTG turbo, refined

economicsRule.title: The E46/5 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£4,500 M54 recon against a £2,000–£8,000 E46/5 sits at 30–225% of the car's value. On a sub‑£4,000 Compact, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £7,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 35–65% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

problems.title: N42/N46 Timing Chain & VANOS Wear
problems.affectedModels: E46/5 316ti, 318ti (2001–2004) with N42 or N46 engine.
problems.rootCause: The N42 and N46 four‑cylinder engines feature a timing chain at the rear of the engine (similar to the later N47) and VANOS variable valve timing — both can wear, leading to rattling on start‑up and reduced performance.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of rattle on start‑up, get the timing chain and VANOS inspected — a £600–£1,200 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,200–£4,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare N42/N46 engine prices for your E46/5 →

problems.title: M54 Cooling System Failure
problems.rootCause: The M54's cooling system — plastic radiator end tanks, ageing hoses, expansion tank and water pump — is the defining weakness of this otherwise robust engine. Overheating leads to head gasket failure.
problems.recommendation: On any 325ti, budget for cooling system inspection at 60k miles — a £200–£500 preventative refresh is cheap insurance against the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows a catastrophic overheat.

cta.label: Compare M54 engine prices for your E46/5 →

problems.title: M47 Diesel Injector & Turbo Wear
problems.rootCause: The M47 diesel's injectors can wear, causing rough idle, poor starting and black smoke. The VTG turbocharger on the 320td can also suffer from actuator wear.
problems.recommendation: If your M47‑powered Compact suffers from rough idle or loss of power, have the injectors and turbo inspected — a £200–£500 service is far cheaper than the £800–£1,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M47 engine prices for your E46/5 →

problems.title: E46/5 Rear Subframe Mount Corrosion (Non‑Engine)
problems.affectedModels: All E46/5 variants, particularly UK‑market cars.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but a known structural issue on E46‑based cars — the rear subframe mounting points can corrode or crack, particularly on UK‑market cars exposed to road salt.
problems.recommendation: Have the rear subframe mounts inspected on any E46/5 — it's a common MOT failure point on UK‑market cars.

problems.title: E46/5 Tailgate Wiring Loom Failure (Non‑Engine)
problems.affectedModels: All E46/5 variants, particularly early 2001–2003 models.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but a common non‑engine issue on hatchback BMWs — the wiring loom in the tailgate hinge area can fracture, causing rear light failure, central locking issues, and boot release problems.
problems.recommendation: Have the tailgate wiring inspected on any E46/5 — it's a common age‑related failure that can cause MOT issues if left unaddressed.

replacementCosts.h2: E46/5 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — E46/5 shares similar engine‑bay access with the E46 saloon and touring, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required. All costs are [BMW-QUOTE] from Table A — Engine Replacement Economics.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are BMW 3 Series Compact E46/5 (E46‑based) only — 3‑door hatchback body style. E46 saloon, coupé, touring and convertible are covered on their own Generation Pages.
